parentQuestion: A patient presents with severe acne. Examination reveals sebaceous glands that are overfilled with sebum, causing them to rupture and trigger inflammation. What mode of secretion characterizes these problematic glands?

scaffolds.q: Sebaceous glands produce an oily substance called sebum.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Sebum lubricates and waterproofs your skin and hair.

scaffolds.q: Acne occurs when sebaceous glands produce too much sebum.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Excess oil + blocked pores = acne. The sebum has nowhere to go.

scaffolds.q: In HOLOCRINE secretion, the cell fills with product and then ruptures.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The cell literally explodes to release its contents. The cell dies in the process.

scaffolds.q: Sebaceous glands use holocrine secretion - the cells rupture to release sebum.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Each sebaceous cell swells with oil until it bursts. Cellular martyrdom for soft skin.

scaffolds.q: MEROCRINE secretion keeps the cell intact - the cell doesn't rupture.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Sweat glands use merocrine secretion. The cells survive and keep secreting.

scaffolds.q: APOCRINE secretion pinches off part of the cell, but doesn't destroy it completely.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Some mammary glands work this way. Partial loss, not total destruction.

scaffolds.q: In acne, holocrine secretion creates lots of dead cell debris along with excess sebum.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Cell rupture = cell death debris. This debris gets trapped in blocked pores and feeds bacteria.

scaffolds.q: Sebaceous glands use HOLOCRINE secretion - cells rupture and die to release sebum.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! This normal process goes wrong in acne when ducts get blocked.

scaffolds.q: The answer is HOLOCRINE secretion - the mode where cells rupture and die.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Not merocrine (cell stays intact), not apocrine (only apex lost), not endocrine (that's hormone secretion into blood).
